speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, July 9th, 1995, interview with Maurice Myers, in Flushing, New York, United States. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] OK, Mr. Myers. Why don't you tell us about your last hiding space in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks to this woman, I got this place in Grodzisk, Kilinskiego Street. This was a big sod. It belonged to two old sisters-- old maids. There was a-- a displaced Polack from Poznan, which he was very poor. So they gave-- and this was a-- a one room house in the middle of this sod. And they gave him 50% of the-- of the potatoes he had, and apples. Bread, he didn't have money to buy. Potatoes and apples, he had all year around. And this he was living on. And somebody recommended me there. This woman recommended me there. And he was very happy to have me there, because I start paying him money. And then him-- I-- over there, I could always get out through the sod, between the trees, and nobody could see me-- tall trees. And-- and I was there already, enjoying the last few months of the war. After being there a few months, I still had to pay him, but the money start to be short.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was a young boy, in my house, where I was living, was a guy who made cigarettes-- believe it or not-- homemade cigarettes. And I saw how they make these cigarettes. I told to the guy, go out on the market. Buy me a couple of kilo cut-- Tobacco.

speaker: interviewee
text: --tobacco. And buy these paper boxes. There were 500 in a box. And I will make cigarette. You will sell the cigarette. And we'll make a living! He bought me this-- these-- he brought me a-- a couple of kilo, a whole table of this cut tobacco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he bought me the paper. I made about 3,000 pieces a day. I start at 7:00 in the morning, till 1 o'clock. I was through till 2 o'clock for 3,000. I pressed them at the oven, nicely. They were-- the moment he took the 5 or 6 cartons with-- with 500 pieces through the first store, they ask him when he's gonna have again. They could sell these cigarettes faster than I could make. And we started to make a beautiful living on these cigarettes. We shared the business. He was supplying me the merchandise and selling. And I was producing. So I produced very nicely. Every day I was working. And we made a comfortable living. We bought a little pig. I bought the pig. He-- he raised it. And for the-- for the holidays, I went with him with this guy for the holidays to the-- to church. And I was like a part of the family. Naturally, he knew who I am, his wife. But the neighbors or-- didn't know. And it was working good. Matter of fact, we had across the street was the city engineer by the name of Pashkovsky. His wife was a very nice lady, and she saw my face. And she saw that I am Jewish. At night she came in. Stephan, come out for a walk. You sit the whole day in the house. I'll take you out for a walk. I know what happened. All her family is 1/2 Jewish. Her family married a law-- famous Jewish lawyers. In Warsaw was a name by Vinaver, one of the famous Jewish families which had already mixed Jewish/Polish. And if somebody will know before the war, Poland had one Jew in the government by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. He changed his name, because he became a Christian. This was a brother of this Mr. Vinaver, which was her family, her sister's family. And I got acquainted with them at night. They were living a mile away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at night, it was dark. She-- she took us for a walk to their home. It was called Milanowek, near Grodzisk, near my town. And we had beautiful conversations with them-- highly intelligent, educated people. This was the-- the-- the class Jews which Polish Jewry had the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got to know with them over there. So the last few month, we were already 1/2 free, because we saw what was going on. And it came June, '44. The Russians came through the Vistula, through the river. And if you had glasses-- binoculars, you could see the Russian soldier. The-- the Polacks made the uprising. Because they thought, this is it. The Germans came back to Warsaw, arrest-- made a lot of arrests and send them again to prisons and to Auschwitz. This director, from [NON-ENGLISH] 34, which was hiding me, came to me, because I was in touch with him. He was nice people. I gave him my bed, because he was displaced from his beautiful apartment--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Warsaw. He came to me to Grodzisk. I gave him my bed-- my respects, because he was an old and gray-haired man-- such a lovely people and his wife. And we were friends after-- even after the war we were in touch for a certain time till the war ends. It was-- then was January 18. We had also built in this sod, a bunker-- underground. I met there another Jewish couple which were hiding with their family-- mixed-- which married-- another married Jewish and non-Jewish. And we became friends. We-- we built a bunker. January 18, 1945, our landlord, my landlord, my-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name-- he came. Stephan, you are a free man. The Russians are here. Naturally, the Russian trucks start to-- to move in through town. We were all running through the city. Suddenly, I became a free man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel free? Did you know this was happening? I mean, what was the feeling when the Soviet army came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The feeling was like this. I went, and the whole town was in the street. The whole city, the whole Grodzisk was in the street, greeting them, and-- and one-- one-- and they were moving in these truck. Probably they moved around, because it's impossible they should have for five-- five hours, only tanks, and tanks, and tanks. Probably they came back and moved around to show the power or the strength. And one tankist went down in the uniform. And he said-- and I-- I told him in Russian, you know, I am Jewish. He says, don't tell me you are Jewish. If you would be Jewish, you wouldn't be alive any more. You are not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I'm not Jewish. What can I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when I-- and then, when I came back home, I-- I-- I lied down, and I started to cry for an hour. I couldn't-- I couldn't help myself. She was kissing me, the-- the-- my landlady. Stephan, you are gonna be a free man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard for me then-- very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then after, after a day, I was staying again, another day with them. I started my walk to Lodz. Why to Lodz? Because Lodz was the big textile town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go back to my-- to my city. I-- I know I have nobody there. I have nothing. And in Lodz, I know my uncle's apartment. I knew. And he had only a son. I came to Ogrodowa five, and Lodz. And a matter of fact, the super thought that I am his son. Oh, I have your apartment. Here is the key. Come up, and this. | knew that apartment where I was there before the war in Lodz, a few times at my uncle's apartment-- beautiful furnished apartment-- a beautiful painting of Adolf Hitler. It was a high officer living here. You should see the furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a clock standing from here to there-- one, two, three bedrooms-- one, two, three bedrooms, a living room, a kitchen. In the kitchen-- in the kitchen I had potatoes, and sugar, and salt, and-- and whatever you want, and-- and-- and beddings, and-- and every-- everything! What happened? They, one night, when they felt the Russians are coming, they disappeared. What did you do with the painting of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: The painting of Hitler, I-- I took the first day-- right away I took it down. I took it out of the frame. I put it in the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bathroom. And when I sat down and I kept my legs, my feet, my shoes on him and-- and-- and thinking, what did he do to our life? But I wish I would keep this painting today. It's worth, I understand, $100,000. [LAUGHS] Now when did you-- you never went back to Ostrowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- and then-- I'll tell you a little story after, if I have still time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, I was in Lodz. You don't have no money, no food, no bread. So there was a Lodz ghetto left over from the Lodz ghetto Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they baked right away bread. This was-- and everybody, we start to meet Jews-- survivors. And what was the street? I forgot the street already. We went there for bread-- a bread. I went there, and whom did I meet? My boyfriend from my-- from my town-- a very good friend of mine. He says, what are you doing here? I said, the same thing what are you doing. I-- I need a bread. I came for-- are you kidding, he says? You came here for a bread? Come with me!

speaker: interviewee
text: I came with the Russian army from Lublin, from the east side. He was with the Russian. He was with the Russian army-- his best friend Russian officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me to an apartment-- to his apartment. And he opened one room, was laying from the top to the bottom, till the ceiling-- full of merchandise-- textiles, socks, hose-- whatever you want. What-- how did you get all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask me a question. I have officers. We went to factories-- cleaned up factories. Because the German left, and there was nobody. It-- it was-- everybody grabbed whatever. And he has-- and he says, sit down. You're gonna have lunch with me. He took out Nescafe-- Nescafe-- American Nescafe, with-- with the-- with milk-- canned milk-- canned food. I said, I didn't see this for five years. He says, you are a businessman. You remember your father had a store. Start selling me these goods. I'm not a businessman. I don't know the prices. I don't know anything. Well, I said, but I don't have money. He says, you take. And if you sell, you bring me the money. I started to take two valises packed up with merchandise. And I started. The-- the first thing-- I want to see my town. I went to Ostrowiec. He had also Ciba-- Cibazol [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was [NON-ENGLISH]? And Coramine-- Coramine was before the war, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] If somebody had a heart trouble, they didn't make bypasses there. She took drops of Coramine, and this helped her to move whatever. And the Cibazol-- Ciba-- was like-- like aspirin, you know. He gave me a liter of this-- of this Coramine, a pound, or kilo, of this Ciba-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This was like 10,000 Bayer's Aspirin. I came to my town. And there were two drugstores. I came to the first drugstore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello, there. Oh, Mr. Myers, you are this. Hello. How are you? Do you know my father and my mother? Nobody's alive. What do you have? I say I have a Ciba. How much do you-- how much does it cost? He told me the price. Then if I would say 5,000 zlotys, I told her 10,000 zlotys. She said, when are you gonna have-- be again? She-- if-- if-- if-- if somebody wanted 10 pills, she gave 3 pills to her customer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I gave her, maybe 1,000 or 2,000-- I don't know how much it was in this box-- she started to sell it. When are you-- please, don't go to anybody. Come again to me. The bottle-- and I sold right away for double the price. I made double the money. I paid off everybody. I paid. And I was in business already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now since-- in the time after you came to the United States, what did you make a-- what kind of career did you make for yourself here?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few-- a few month-- it took only a few month later in April. I rented a store. I opened a store. In April, '45 I had already a store, and I start to do business. The point is how to get merchandise. How to sell was no problem. How to get-- I got a legal license. I was a wholesaler from textile. My sister worked in the government, in the textile industry. She became right away a director, because she had 8 or-- or 10 year education, so. And-- and in Poland, by the communists, the intelligentsia didn't want to work for the government, because they stayed away. They were waiting from London, the government. And they don't want to work for the communist. So naturally, anybody who had-- who knew how to write and read got the job for the government. And she helped me getting this license. And she helped me getting this amount of merchandise.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you moved here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: But, I mean, what did you do when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was back in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was back in Poland. From Poland, I made a few thousand dollars. I-- I had to run away. I went, because he told me that the-- the director of the-- of the IRS was my best friend. We were dining every week and drinking. He tell me, you remember, there's a communist government. You want to become a capitalist. You're going to be in jail soon. So I said, I lived through such a war with Hitler-- to go to a Russian jail. I disappeared. I-- in those days, there was a Bricha from Israel. They took over all the-- most, and practically 90% of the Jews-- of the leftover Jews from Poland, through Czechoslovakia. All the borders were open. Through Czechoslovakia to Austria, I went to Mannheim-- to Munich. In Munich I-- I-- I settled down. I was waiting to go to the United States. I had to wait there till 1949. In 1949, the CIC came to my house and told me, I have-- this was the Truman quota. I was one of the first one to be in the Truman quota. And I was happy to pack up the things and run to-- through Bremerhaven, with the Ernie Pyle, a military boat, which was a 700 or 800 ton boat-- a very small boat. Naturally, my wife was scared to death. Since then, she never went on a boat, and she never saw a boat. She's scared to look at a boat. That the nights, what we had there with the storms-- it was a-- we were 14 days from-- 14 days. And I was laying 14 days. I didn't-- I couldn't sit up for 14 day. I was going up. And my wife felt good, but she was scared to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we arrived on the 22nd of February, 1949. It was Washington's birthday. We were greeted by the mayor of Boston. And from Boston, I came to New York. I still had an uncle in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked in business here too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I got an apartment. And after a few months walking around and looking around, I-- I got a job at NSL products, 339 Broadway, a textile house. It was in April, May. In July, my son was born, because my wife came here already pregnant. And naturally, it was everything tough, not knowing the language, not knowing a doctor, not knowing where to go, and how to go, and what to do. I was lucky. I had this Dr. Goldfeber, my cousin, which he helped me, practically, to go to a doctor, and-- and things. And January 9-- January 1st, 1950, I opened my store--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you kept it for some--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the first store with a front.

speaker: interviewer
text: --until your retirement?

speaker: interviewee
text: And growing bigger, and bigger, and bigger. And going to-- this was 136 Allen Street. And got one little store, then I get another one, then another one. But still, it was too small. I bought a building on 303 Grand Street. And in 1962, I moved to 303 Grand Street till I sold my whole thing in the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. I'll ask you. Let me ask you one last question before I end this. You went through all this, and came here, and-- this whole production that we've done here and all these accounts of testimonies-- what do you think is the message that should be given about the Holocaust, as one who has survived such an ordeal? What do you think that the people of the future should know about it, or what should the story-- that should be told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think, yes. It's a good question. We should never forget that we are Jews. We should never forget that anti-Semitism exists, in the whole world-- in certain countries more, in certain countries less. Thank God that I am living in the United States at the age where no Jew enjoyed such freedom as we are enjoying in this United States, which everything is open for a Jew. We have representation in-- in Congress. We have a nice representation in Senate. And we are recognized as a minority with the same rights as anybody else. In-- in my life, I didn't enjoy yet such a age, such years, as I live here. And we should not forget that this doesn't come for free. We have to behave, and we have to be always a sample giving. What other people can do, Jews cannot do. Because if a Jew goes to prison, they say he did it, and he goes to prison. If 100 other people goes to prison, they are allowed to. But we are not allowed. And we have to give a-- a sample-- humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: A light onto the nations.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the nation, as we are. A matter of fact, Israel is giving-- Israelis are giving quite a sample in the world what democracy is. And we shouldn't take anything for granted. We shouldn't take it for granted. We should enjoy the freedom and not take it for granted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Myers.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Will you tell us who we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Marie, which I married in 1945, in December, 24, because since I was in business, this was the only day off. And I could marry her. She was a very beautiful girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was lucky to have her. She helped me a lot in my life. And she gave us a nice son by the name of Jay, and two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now the-- the 25th of December this year, it's gonna be 50 years we are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the 24th but the 25th.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my father. Naturally, in the younger years, in his life, thanks to my uncle in Toronto, Canada. He gave me this picture. Otherwise, I would never have his picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in our town in-- in Ostrowiec, in his middle age years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older sister, Rosa. She was to me like my mother, because my mother was always in the store, busy. And she brought us up. She-- she gave us food. She dressed us. And she took care of us till she got married and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: 9-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the-- in the '30s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my picture, taken during the war, on my papers, which I needed-- my false papers. That's the way how I looked during the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you can take-- This picture was a Xerox copy from a book, which a friend of mine from Ostrowiec wrote a book. And he-- this shows me on the extreme right, the first on the right, playing a little cards with my friends from Betar, which we belonged to the same RA youth organization in Ostrowiec. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture represents our wedding night in our own apartment-- and from 1945, December 25--

speaker: interviewee
text: --taken at the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Jay, born in 19-- in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '49, when-- in July 11, when we came to this country not a year, because my wife was pregnant when we came here. He lives in Maplewood, New Jersey, and he's in the leather business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewee
text: This represents me and my wife, and my two grandsons, Cory and Jeremy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Florida, on vacation-- when we were on vacation. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your sons twins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Say-- say they're twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The two boys are twins, born in 1973, November 23. One is in the navy. Jeremy is in the navy, and Cory is in Tampa University.
